title: Azure AI Fundamentals Concepts and Implementation
description: This package covers fundamental AI concepts, including fairness, safety, privacy, and explainability, along with matching AI tasks to relevant Azure AI models.

sources.title: Microsoft Certified: Azure AI Fundamentals (AI-901) Master Cheat Sheet

domains.title: Implement AI solutions by using Microsoft Foundry

concepts.title: AI Fairness: Definition, Bias, and Mitigation
concepts.summary: Fairness in AI means treating all people equitably without bias based on characteristics like race or gender. Key concepts include bias (from data or algorithm), fairness metrics to measure equality, and mitigation strategies to reduce bias. The text contrasts unfair outcomes with fair outcomes, emphasizing that fairness requires proactive steps.

grounding.text: Fairness means that an AI system treats all people equitably, without bias or discrimination based on characteristics like race, gender, age, disability, or socioeconomic status.
Data bias: Training data under-represents or stereotypes groups.
Remember: Fairness is proactive , not automatic. Even unbiased data can produce unfair outcomes.

concepts.title: AI Reliability and Safety Concepts
concepts.summary: Reliability and safety in AI mean the system performs consistently and correctly while failing safely when unexpected conditions arise. Key concepts include robustness, graceful degradation, testing coverage, and defending against adversarial attacks. The text provides examples across self-driving cars and medical diagnosis, contrasting unreliable behavior with reliable, safe responses.

grounding.text: Reliability and safety means that an AI system performs consistently and correctly under all expected conditions, and fails safely without causing harm when unexpected conditions occur.
- Robustness - AI works correctly despite variations in input, noise, or missing data.
Aspect | Unreliable AI | Reliable AI | --- | --- | --- | Response to noise | Erratic or confident wrong answers | Detects uncertainty, asks for clarification

concepts.title: Privacy and Security in AI Systems
concepts.summary: Privacy and security in AI systems involve protecting sensitive data from unauthorized access, respecting user consent, and ensuring regulatory compliance. Key concepts include data minimization, user consent, anonymization/pseudonymization, encryption, and defending against attacks like model inversion. The section also provides practical Azure tools and relevant regulations like GDPR and HIPAA.

grounding.text: Privacy and security means that an AI system protects sensitive data from unauthorized access, respects user consent, and ensures that data is collected, stored, and used in compliance with regulations.
Remove or mask personally identifiable information (PII) so data cannot be traced back to an individual.
Azure Key Vault - Manage encryption keys and secrets.

concepts.title: Considerations for Inclusiveness in AI Solutions
concepts.summary: Inclusiveness means designing AI systems to empower everyone regardless of ability, age, gender, or background. Key concepts include accessibility, universal design, cultural diversity, and co-creation. The text provides examples of inclusive versus non-inclusive AI and lists practical tips and Azure tools for achieving inclusion.

grounding.text: Inclusiveness means that AI systems are designed and built to empower everyone, regardless of ability, age, gender, race, culture, language, or technical skill.
- Accessibility - AI must work for people with disabilities (visual, hearing, motor, cognitive).
- Azure tools for inclusiveness: Azure AI Speech - Supports many languages, accents, and custom pronunciation.

concepts.title: AI Solution Transparency and Explainability
concepts.summary: Transparency means AI systems are open and understandable, requiring users to know how the AI works, what data it uses, and why it makes decisions. Key concepts include explainability, interpretability, disclosure, traceability, and acknowledging limitations. The text details different types of explainability, such as global and local explainability, and lists practical tips for using Azure tools.

grounding.text: Transparency means that AI systems are open and understandable. Users should know when they are interacting with an AI, how it works, what data it uses, and why it makes specific decisions or recommendations.
- Explainability - The ability to explain an AI's output in human terms (why did it say "loan denied").
Global explainability Explains overall model behavior "This model weighs income 60%, credit score 30%, age 10%"

concepts.title: AI System Accountability
concepts.summary: Accountability means human beings are responsible for the design, deployment, operation, and outcomes of an AI system, requiring clear ownership and remediation mechanisms when harm occurs. Key concepts include Human-in-the-loop, Governance, Auditability, and Clear ownership. The section also details the accountability chain, practical Azure tools, and distinguishes accountability from responsibility.

grounding.text: Accountability means that human beings are responsible for the design, deployment, operation, and outcomes of an AI system. If an AI causes harm or makes a mistake, there must be clear ownership, governance, and mechanisms for remediation.
- Human-in-the-loop - Critical decisions should have human review and override capability.
Responsibility | Doing the task (e.g., an engineer builds the model) | Accountability | Owning the outcome (e.g., the manager is answerable if the model fails)

concepts.title: Generative AI Fundamentals and Workflow
concepts.summary: Generative AI creates new content by learning patterns from training data. Key concepts include training data, parameters, tokens, prompts, and the inference process. The workflow involves pre-training, optional fine-tuning, and inference where a prompt is converted into a generated output.

grounding.text: Generative AI is a type of artificial intelligence that creates new content (text, images, code, audio, video) by learning patterns from existing training data.
| Tokens | Small pieces of text (words or sub-words) that the model processes. |
[Input Prompt] → [Tokenize] → [Generative Model (with parameters)] → [Predict next token iteratively] → [Decode] → [Generated Output]

concepts.title: Matching AI Tasks to Azure AI Models
concepts.summary: This section defines the core capabilities of AI models, outlines the main AI task categories (Generative, Predictive, Classification, etc.), and provides a detailed mapping of specific tasks to appropriate Azure AI services (like Azure OpenAI, Azure ML, or specialized services). It also includes a decision flowchart and comparison tips for exam preparation.

grounding.text: Selecting the right Azure AI model means matching the task requirement (what you want the AI to do) with the model's capabilities (what it is designed to do best).
Generative AI Creates new content Write a poem, generate an image
If you need to... Generate text, code, or chat responses Azure OpenAI Service (GPT-4, GPT-3.5) Capability type Generative AI

concepts.title: AI Model Deployment Options and Configuration Parameters
concepts.summary: Deployment options determine where and how an AI model runs, categorized into Azure cloud, local devices, Kubernetes, and IoT Edge. Configuration parameters control model behavior, cost, security, and performance, including generative AI parameters like temperature and top-p, and non-generative parameters like batch size and instance count.

grounding.text: Deployment options determine where and how your AI model runs and is accessible. Configuration parameters control how the model behaves during inference (generating results).

Need real-time response (<1 sec)?

```
Deployment Decision Flowchart (Exam Critical)

       Need real-time response (<1 sec)?
       |
       /- YES -> Real-time endpoint
       |
       \- NO -> Need to process large batches overnight?
           |
           /- YES -> Batch endpoint
           |
           \- NO -> Does device have internet? / Is data too sensitive for cloud?
               |
               /- NO internet OR sensitive data -> IoT Edge / On-premises
               |
               \- Have internet AND need portability -> Container
```

| Category | Parameters | What they control |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature, Top-p, Max tokens, Stop sequences | Creativity, length, where to stop | Inference behavior |

concepts.title: AI Workload Identification and Azure Services
concepts.summary: This section defines an AI workload as a specific problem an AI solution solves and maps various real-world scenarios to corresponding Azure AI services. It details workloads like Generative AI, Agentic AI, Text Analysis, Speech, Computer Vision, and Information Extraction, along with practical keywords for exam differentiation.

grounding.text:
| What it does | Real-world scenario (Exam Focus) | AI Workload Key Azure Service |
|---|---|---|
| Creates new original content (text, images, code, audio) based on patterns learned from training data. | - Chatbot answering questions (Azure OpenAI) - Generating product images from descriptions - Summarizing long documents - Writing code from comments | Generative AI Azure OpenAI Service |

· Keyword to watch for: - 'Create new', 'generate', 'write', 'summarize', 'compose' → Generative AI - 'Act autonomously', 'achieve a goal', 'take action', 'multi-step task' → Agentic AI - 'Pos/neg', 'feeling', 'mood' → Sentiment Analysis (part of Text Analysis) - 'Transcribe', 'convert voice to text' → Speech Recognition - 'Synthesize', 'convert text to voice' → Text-to-Speech - 'Identify objects', 'detect faces', 'read text from image' → Computer Vision - 'Pull data from form/invoice', 'find entities (e.g., Person, Location)' → Information Extraction

concepts.title: Common Text Analysis Techniques (NLP)
concepts.summary: Text analysis (NLP) involves automatically extracting meaning from text using techniques like Keyword Extraction, Entity Detection, Sentiment Analysis, and Summarization. The section details what each technique does, its input/output examples, primary Azure services, and practical tips for mapping scenarios to the correct technique.

grounding.text: Text analysis (also called Natural Language Processing or NLP) is the process of automatically extracting meaningful information from unstructured text.
Identifies the most important words or short phrases that summarize the main topics of a text.
Determines the emotional tone or opinion expressed in text (positive, negative, neutral, or mixed).

concepts.title: Speech Recognition vs. Speech Synthesis Features
concepts.summary: Speech AI has two core capabilities: Speech Recognition (listening/comprehension, converting Audio to Text) and Speech Synthesis (speaking/generation, converting Text to Audio). This section details the specific features, technical names, and use cases for both STT and TTS, including concepts like SSML, custom voices, and diarization, along with practical exam tips for differentiation.

grounding.text: Speech AI enables applications to process human voice. Two core capabilities exist: Speech Recognition (listening/comprehension) and Speech Synthesis (speaking/generation).

| Feature | What it means | Example Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time transcription Processes audio as it latency) | arrives (low Live meeting captions, voice commands |
Exam Tip: If you hear "transcribe," "captions," "convert voice to text," "real-time transcription," "speaker diarization" → think Speech Recognition .

concepts.title: Computer Vision vs. Image Generation Capabilities
concepts.summary: This section details the core capabilities of Computer Vision (analysis, including tagging, OCR, object detection, and face analysis) and Image Generation (creating new visuals via DALL-E). It provides a comparison chart, key distinctions (like face detection vs. recognition), and common exam scenarios to help differentiate between the services.

grounding.text: Computer vision enables AI to interpret and understand the visual world from images and video. Image generation creates new visual content from text descriptions or other images.
Detects faces + attributes (age, emotion, glasses) | Azure AI Face service (Face recognition) | Identifies or verifies specific individuals
Task | Azure Service | Key phrase to recognize | Generate image from text | Azure OpenAI (DALL-E) | "create", "generate", "draw", "DALL-E"

concepts.title: Azure AI Information Extraction by Modality
concepts.summary: This section details the primary Azure AI services and key techniques required to extract structured data from text, images, audio, video, and documents. It provides specific examples of what can be extracted (e.g., NER from text, OCR from images, transcription from audio) and highlights the unified solution for video analysis.

grounding.text: One Goal, Multiple Techniques
Azure AI Language | Entities, key phrases, sentiment, PII, summaries | "Named Entity Recognition (NER)" | Text

The "Modality" Trick: When you see a question about extraction, first identify the input type: Text → Azure AI Language, Image → Azure AI Vision (OCR/tags/objects) OR Document Intelligence (if it's a form/invoice)...

concepts.title: Creating Effective Generative AI Prompts
concepts.summary: A prompt is the input text instructing a Generative AI model to produce a desired output, serving as the primary interface in platforms like Microsoft Foundry. Effective prompts consist of high-level instructions (setting rules) and a specific user request. The CTAO framework (Clear & Specific, Task Decomposition, Assign a Role, Output Formatting) is recommended for creation, along with tips on system prompt length and temperature settings.

grounding.text: A prompt is the input text given to a Generative AI model to instruct it to produce a desired output.
Use the CTAO framework for exam questions:
System Prompt Length: Max ~8,000 characters (varies by model). Keep it concise.

concepts.title: Model Deployment and Interaction in Foundry
concepts.summary: Model deployment is the process of making a trained AI model available via an API endpoint in Microsoft Foundry. Key concepts include the Model Catalog, Deployment, Endpoint, Inference, and Token. The guide details the steps to deploy a model, how to interact with it using the Chat or Completions Playground, and provides formulas for token estimation and rate limit monitoring.

grounding.text: Model deployment is the process of making a trained or pre-trained generative AI model available for real-time inference (prediction/response generation) via an API endpoint. In Microsoft Foundry (formerly Azure AI Studio), you deploy models from the Model Catalog to an endpoint for testing, integration, or application use.
| Token | The smallest unit of text the model processes (~4 characters or 0.75 words). | Cost &limit measurement unit |

System Message fi eld: Enter behavior rules (e.g., "You are a helpful math tutor")
User Message fi eld: Enter the user prompt

concepts.title: Building a Lightweight Chat Client with Foundry SDK
concepts.summary: This section defines a lightweight chat client as a minimal program using the Foundry SDK to interact with deployed models programmatically. It details key terminology like the SDK, EndpointURL, and API Key, lists prerequisites, and provides a complete Python code example demonstrating how to install the SDK, configure authentication, create a client, send messages, and print the response.

grounding.text: A lightweight chat client application is a minimal program (console app, script, or basic web app) that uses the Foundry SDK (Azure AI Inference SDK) to send prompts to a deployed model and receive responses programmatically - not through the portal's Playground.
A library of code (Python, C#, JavaScript) that simplifies calling Azure APIs.

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=endpoint,
    credential=credential,
    deployment="my-gpt4-assistant"  # Your deployment name from 2.1.2
)

concepts.title: Creating and Testing Single-Agent Solutions in Foundry
concepts.summary: This section defines an AI agent, distinguishes it from a simple chat model, and provides a detailed, step-by-step workflow for creating a single-agent solution in the Foundry portal. It covers configuring basic settings, adding various tools (like Bing Search and APIs), incorporating knowledge grounding, and testing the agent's reasoning process.

grounding.text: An agent in Microsoft Foundry is an AI system that combines a generative AI model with tools , knowledge sources , and autonomous decision-making to perform tasks.

Click Add tools → Choose from:

| ToolName | WhatIt Does | ExampleUseCase |
|---|---|---|
| Bing Search | Searches the live web for current information | "What are today's shipping delays?" |

| Feature | Chat Playground (2.1.2) | Agent Playground (2.1.4) |
|---|---|---|
| Tools available | No | Yes (Bing, Functions, APIs, Code) |
| Grounding/Knowledge | No | Yes (upload files, connect data) |

concepts.title: Lightweight Agent Client Application
concepts.summary: A lightweight client application is a program that communicates with an agent programmatically, differing from a Chat Client by focusing on agent capabilities and tool execution. Key concepts include the Agent Client SDK, conversation threads, runs, and agent IDs. The section provides prerequisites, a basic Python implementation flow, and an exam-ready code pattern for handling agent execution and tool calls.

grounding.text: A lightweight client application for an agent is a program (console app, script, or basic web app) that communicates with an agent (created in 2.1.4) programmatically - not through the portal's Agent Playground.
The library (azure-ai-projects) for programmatically interacting with an agent.

Step 1: Install the Agent SDK
bash
pip install azure-ai-projects

concepts.title: Core Text Analysis Techniques and Foundry Application Setup
concepts.summary: Text analysis involves extracting meaning, insights, and structured information from unstructured text through techniques like Keyword Extraction, Entity Detection, Sentiment Analysis, and Summarization. Building an application in Foundry requires setting up a project, choosing a model, and using the Foundry SDK in Python to call the model's API endpoint.

grounding.text: Text analysis involves extracting meaning, insights, and structured information from unstructured text.
| Identifies important terms or phrases | From "The newAzure AI model is fast and accurate." -> Keywords: "Azure AI model", "fast", "accurate" | Keyword Extraction |

1. Setup: Use Azure Foundry portal to create a project. 2. Choose Model/Service: Select a suitable model from the model catalog (e.g., GPT-4, Llama, or specialized language models). 3. Client Application (Python): Use the Foundry SDK to call the model via its API endpoint.

concepts.title: Multimodal Model Input and Processing
concepts.summary: Multimodal models can process various inputs like text, images, and audio. When a spoken prompt (audio file) is sent, the model transcribes it (if necessary) and generates a text or audio response. Common applications include voice assistants and real-time translation. Implementation involves deploying a model, preparing audio input, and using a Chat Completion API.

grounding.text: Multimodal models can process and respond to multiple types of input (modalities) like text, images, and audio .
How it works: You send a spoken prompt (audio file) to a deployed multimodal model. The model transcribes the audio (if needed) and generates a relevant text or audio response.
Note: Multimodal models extend beyond text, allowing you to send audio files directly in prompts for the model to process and respond to.

concepts.title: Azure Speech Core Capabilities and Application Workflow
concepts.summary: Azure Speech offers two main capabilities: Speech Recognition (Speech-to-Text) to convert audio to text, and Speech Synthesis (Text-to-Speech) to convert text into audio. Applications can be built using the Azure Speech SDK within Foundry projects, following a five-step workflow: Capture, Recognize, Process, Synthesize, and Playback.

grounding.text: Azure Speech provides two core capabilities for building speech-enabled applications.
Speech Recognition | Speech-to-Text | Converts spoken audio into written text

1. Capture: Record user's voice. 2. Recognize (Speech-to-Text): Use the API to transcribe the audio to text. 3. Process: Send the transcribed text to a language model to generate a response (e.g., answer a question). 4. Synthesize (Text-to-Speech): Use the API to convert the text response into audio. 5. Playback: Play the audio response to the user.

concepts.title: Multimodal Model Capabilities and Implementation
concepts.summary: Multimodal models can analyze images to perform tasks like captioning, answering visual questions, detecting objects, and reading text. Implementation involves deploying a vision-capable model and sending the image data (via URL or base64) along with a specific instruction in the user prompt.

grounding.text: Similar to processing audio, multimodal models can analyze images to answer questions, describe content, or extract information.

- Image captioning and description
- Visual question answering ("What color is the car?")
- Object detection and counting
- Reading text from images

1. Deploy a vision-capable multimodal model (e.g., GPT-4 with vision, Phi-4multimodal).
2. Send the image as a URL or base64-encoded data in the user prompt.
3. Ask a specific question or give an instruction about the image.

concepts.title: Generative Model Image Creation Workflow
concepts.summary: Generative models can create new images from natural language text prompts, supporting text-to-image generation, image editing, and video creation. In Microsoft Foundry, users interact with models like DALL-E or Stable Diffusion via APIs to follow a workflow: select a model, craft a prompt, call the API, and receive the output.

grounding.text: Generative models can create new images based on natural language text prompts.

1. Select Model: Deploy an image generation model. 2. Craft Prompt: Write a detailed prompt describing the desired image (style, subject, setting, mood). 3. Call API: Send the prompt to the model's endpoint. 4. Receive Output: The API returns the generated image (e.g., as a URL or binary data).

concepts.title: Vision Application Architecture and Extension
concepts.summary: A vision application combines interpretation (analyzing an image to return text) and generation (creating an image based on a text prompt) into a single application. The architecture involves a frontend for user input, a backend (Foundry) to handle multimodal model analysis, and an output display. Extending this involves using an image generation model when the user requests a new image.

grounding.text: This involves combining both interpretation and generation (or just one) into a single application.
1. Frontend: A simple web or command-line interface where a user can upload an image and ask a question.
The app sends the image and question to a deployed multimodal model . The model analyzes the image and returns a text answer.

concepts.title: Azure Content Understanding Overview and Capabilities
concepts.summary: Azure Content Understanding is a multimodal AI service that transforms unstructured content (documents, forms, images, audio, video) into structured, machine-readable data by combining OCR with generative AI reasoning. It supports various content extraction capabilities, including text, selection marks, barcodes, and complex table structures. The service offers three field extraction methods (Extract, Classify, Generate) and provides prebuilt and custom analyzers for specific document types, along with key benefits like intelligent search and confidence-driven automation.

grounding.text: Azure Content Understanding is a preview multimodal AI service that transforms unstructured content (documents, forms, images, audio, video) into structured, machine-readable data.
Field Extraction The process of extracting, classifying, or generating specific data points from documents (e.g., invoice number, date, total amount)
Primary Focus "Laser-focused extraction"-OCR + layout Azure AI Document Intelligence "Extraction + reasoning in one pass"

concepts.title: Azure Content Understanding for Image Extraction
concepts.summary: Azure Content Understanding extends multimodal extraction to still images by combining computer vision (object detection, OCR) with generative AI reasoning, handling unstructured visual content. It supports various extractions like printed text, objects, faces, logos, and charts, and offers specialized analyzers for different image types. The service automatically detects the content modality (document vs. image) to apply appropriate preprocessing.

grounding.text: Azure Content Understanding extends its multimodal extraction capabilities to still images (photographs, scanned documents, screenshots, diagrams, etc.). Unlike document-focused extraction that assumes structured layouts, image extraction handles unstructured visual content by combining computer vision (object detection, OCR) with generative AI reasoning.

Printed Text (OCR) Text visible in images
Handwritten notes in images Post-it notes, forms, handwritten captions

concepts.title: Azure Content Understanding: Audio and Video Processing
concepts.summary: Azure Content Understanding extends to temporal modalities like audio and video, combining speech-to-text, event detection, and frame analysis with generative AI. It defines key terms such as Speaker Diarization and Temporal Grounding, details what can be extracted from both audio and video, and compares the processing characteristics of the two modalities.

grounding.text: Azure Content Understanding extends to temporal modalities -audio and video processing spoken language, ambient sounds, speaker identification, and visualtemporal cues.

Every extracted piece of information includes timestamp grounding: { "field": "ActionItem", "value": "Submit Q3 report by Friday", "grounding": { "startTime": "00:15:32", "endTime": "00:15:47", "speaker": "Speaker B", "confidence": 0.94 } }

| Aspect | Audio-Only | Video |
|---|---|---|
| Yes (primary) | Yes (from audio track) | Speech Transcription |

questions.stem: What does fairness in AI mean?

options.text: An AI system that maximizes profit for the majority group.
options.rationale: This is incorrect because fairness requires equitable treatment without bias.

options.text: Treating all people equitably without bias based on characteristics like race or gender.
options.rationale: This directly matches the definition provided.

options.text: Ensuring that the training data is perfectly balanced across all demographic groups.
options.rationale: While data balance is related to bias, it is not the definition of AI fairness itself.

options.text: Implementing algorithms that only consider age and disability in decision-making.
options.rationale: Fairness requires consideration of characteristics like race, gender, age, disability, or socioeconomic status, not limiting consideration to a subset.

questions.explanation: Fairness in AI means that an AI system treats all people equitably, without bias or discrimination based on characteristics like race, gender, age, disability, or socioeconomic status.
hints: Focus on the core concept of equitable treatment.
Consider what characteristics fairness must account for.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a basis for bias in AI systems?

options.rationale: Income level. While socioeconomic status is a factor, it is not the only basis.
Race or gender. Race and gender are characteristics that serve as a basis for bias.
Programming language. Programming language is not a basis for bias.
System uptime. System uptime is not related to the basis for bias.

questions.explanation: Bias in AI systems can be based on characteristics such as race, gender, age, disability, or socioeconomic status.
hints: Review the list of characteristics mentioned in the definition of fairness.
Look for specific examples of protected characteristics.

questions.stem: What is data bias defined as in the context of AI?

options.text: The process of mitigating all algorithmic errors.
options.rationale: Data bias is specifically about the training data itself, not the mitigation process.

options.text: Training data that under-represents or stereotypes groups.
options.rationale: Data bias is defined as training data under-representing or stereotyping groups.

options.text: The final decision made by the deployed AI model.
options.rationale: This describes the outcome, not the source of the bias.

options.text: The act of proactively ensuring fairness after the model is built.
options.rationale: Fairness is proactive, but data bias is a specific issue within the data used for training.

questions.explanation: Data bias occurs when the training data under-represents or stereotypes certain groups.
hints: Identify the specific definition provided for 'Data bias'.
Think about what happens to groups in the training data.

questions.stem: True or False: Even if the training data is unbiased, an AI system can still produce unfair outcomes.

options.rationale: Even unbiased data can produce unfair outcomes.
This contradicts the text, which emphasizes that fairness is proactive, not automatic.

questions.explanation: Fairness is proactive, not automatic; therefore, even unbiased data can lead to unfair outcomes.
hints: Recall the specific statement regarding unbiased data and outcomes.
Remember the emphasis on proactive fairness.

questions.stem: Which of the following concepts are key components related to achieving AI fairness?

options.text: Identifying bias in the training data.
options.rationale: Identifying bias in the training data.

options.text: Using fairness metrics to measure equality.
options.rationale: Using fairness metrics to measure equality.

options.text: Ensuring the algorithm is perfectly neutral to all input features.
options.rationale: Implementing mitigation strategies to reduce bias.

options.text: Implementing mitigation strategies to reduce bias.
options.rationale: Assuming that if data is clean, the outcome will automatically be fair.

options.text: Assuming that if data is clean, the outcome will automatically be fair.
options.rationale: Ensuring the algorithm is perfectly neutral to all input features.

questions.explanation: Achieving AI fairness involves identifying bias in the data, using metrics to measure equality, and applying mitigation strategies to reduce bias.
hints: Consider the three main pillars mentioned in the summary: bias, metrics, and mitigation.
Think about what actions are required to move from an unfair system to a fair one.

questions.stem: What is the definition of reliability and safety in an AI system?

options.text: The AI system must always produce the most optimal output, even if it means making an error.
options.rationale: This contradicts the requirement that the system must perform consistently and correctly.

options.text: The AI system performs consistently and correctly under all expected conditions, and fails safely without causing harm when unexpected conditions occur.
options.rationale: This directly matches the definition provided for reliability and safety.

options.text: The AI system must actively detect and correct all forms of adversarial attacks before they cause harm.
options.rationale: While defending against attacks is a safety concept, it is not the definition of overall reliability and safety.

options.text: The AI system must exhibit robustness against all forms of input noise without requiring any human intervention.
options.rationale: Robustness is a component, but the definition covers both consistent performance and safe failure.

questions.explanation: Reliability and safety means that an AI system performs consistently and correctly under all expected conditions, and fails safely without causing harm when unexpected conditions occur.

questions.stem: Which concept describes an AI working correctly despite variations in input, noise, or missing data?

options.rationale: Graceful degradation relates to how the system handles failure, not its resilience to input variations.
Adversarial defense specifically addresses attacks, not general input variations.
Robustness is defined as AI working correctly despite variations in input, noise, or missing data.
Consistent performance is part of reliability, but robustness specifically addresses handling variations.

questions.explanation: Robustness is defined as AI working correctly despite variations in input, noise, or missing data.

questions.stem: True or False: A reliable AI system is characterized by erratic or confident wrong answers when faced with noise.

options.rationale: Erratic or confident wrong answers indicate an unreliable AI system, whereas reliability requires detecting uncertainty.
A reliable AI system detects uncertainty and asks for clarification, contrasting with erratic answers.

questions.explanation: False. A reliable AI system detects uncertainty and asks for clarification, contrasting with erratic answers.

questions.stem: Which of the following are key concepts related to ensuring AI reliability and safety?

options.rationale: Graceful degradation is a key concept for handling unexpected conditions safely.
Defending against adversarial attacks is a key concept for safety.
Erratic or confident wrong answers describe unreliable AI behavior, not a safety concept.
Robustness is a key concept.

questions.explanation: The key concepts include Graceful degradation, defending against adversarial attacks, and Robustness.

questions.stem: If an AI system encounters unexpected conditions, what is the required response to maintain reliability and safety?

options.text: It should continue processing with the current output, hoping the condition resolves itself.
options.rationale: Continuing processing without adaptation violates the requirement to fail safely.

options.text: It must fail safely without causing harm.
options.rationale: The definition requires the system to fail safely when unexpected conditions occur.

options.text: It should immediately halt all operations and report an error code.
options.rationale: While halting might be a form of safe failure, the core requirement is failing safely, not just halting.

options.text: It must ask for clarification before proceeding.
options.rationale: Asking for clarification is a behavior associated with reliable AI when uncertainty is detected, not the mandatory response to an unexpected condition.

questions.explanation: If an AI system encounters unexpected conditions, it must fail safely without causing harm.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a core requirement for privacy and security in AI systems?

options.rationale: Model inversion defense is a concept, but the core requirements listed are protecting data from unauthorized access, respecting consent, and ensuring regulatory compliance.

options.text: Ensuring data is collected, stored, and used in compliance with regulations.
options.rationale: Privacy and security means ensuring data is collected, stored, and used in compliance with regulations.

options.text: Managing encryption keys using Azure Key Vault.
options.rationale: Managing encryption keys is a specific tool mentioned, not a core requirement of the privacy and security definition itself.

options.text: Removing or masking personally identifiable information (PII) so data cannot be traced back to an individual.
options.rationale: Removing or masking PII is a technique related to privacy, but the definition encompasses broader security and compliance aspects.

questions.explanation: Privacy and security in AI systems involve protecting sensitive data from unauthorized access, respecting user consent, and ensuring that data is collected, stored, and used in compliance with regulations.
hints: Focus on the three main components mentioned in the definition.
Consider what the definition states about data handling.

questions.stem: What action is described as a method to remove or mask personally identifiable information (PII)?

options.text: Using Azure Key Vault to manage encryption keys.

findings.detail: The coverage seems balanced across the two domains (14 concepts each), but without the full question set, it's hard to confirm no orphaned concepts.

The difficulty distribution is heavily skewed towards lower difficulties (1 and 2 combined account for 56% of questions). The Bloom distribution shows a strong emphasis on 'recall' (65%) and 'understand' (43%), with very low representation in higher-order thinking skills like 'apply' (7%) and 'analyze' (20%). This might lead to a lower-level assessment overall.
No immediate signs of near-duplicate clusters based on the sample, but this requires full set review.
The sample questions appear well-formed with clear stems and standard question types. Answer keys are not visible, but the structure seems consistent.
